as: “where an apparently neutral provision, criterion or 
practice would put persons of one sex at a particular 
disadvantage compared with persons of the other sex, 
unless that provision, criterion or practice is objectively 
justified by a legitimate aim, and the means of achieving 
that aim are appropriate and necessary.”
443. In Griggs v. Duke Power Co. MANU/USSC/0066/1971: 
401 U.S. 424 (1971), the US Supreme Court, whilst 
recognizing that African-Americans received sub-standard 
education due to segregated schools, opined that the 
requirement of an aptitude/intelligence test disproportionately 
affected African-American candidates. The Court held that 
“The Civil Rights Act” proscribes not only overt discrimination 
but also practices that are fair in form, but discriminatory 
in operation.”
444. In Bilka-Kaufhaus GmbH v. Karin Weber von Hartz 
(1986) ECR 1607, the European Court of Justice held that 
denying pensions to part-time employees is more likely
